era_context: The mid-15th century continues with the Ottoman Empire consolidating power in the Balkans, European states engaged in the final phases of the Hundred Years' War, and the Italian Renaissance flourishing. The Ming Dynasty is at its height in China; Portuguese maritime exploration is accelerating; and the Inca Empire is expanding in South America.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asian and Steppe polities (sparse written records in European sources)
Pacific Islander societies (no contemporary written records)
Southeast Asian kingdoms (limited documentation in European sources)

events.title: Conquest of Naples by Alfonso V of Aragon
events.description: Alfonso V of Aragon, known as 'Alfonso the Magnanimous,' completes his conquest of the Kingdom of Naples after a prolonged military campaign. He defeats the Angevin claimants and establishes Aragonese rule over Naples, uniting it with Sicily under Aragonese control. This conquest marks a significant shift in Mediterranean power dynamics and establishes the Crown of Aragon as a major Mediterranean power. Alfonso's court in Naples becomes a centre of Renaissance culture and patronage.
key_figures: Alfonso V of Aragon (King of Aragon and Sicily)
events.certainty_note: Alfonso V's conquest of Naples is well-documented in Aragonese, Neapolitan, and Venetian sources. The date of 1442 is firmly established.
cross_references: Part of broader Aragonese expansion in the Mediterranean
Led to 1479-001 (Union of Aragon and Castile)

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet activities
events.description: Admiral Zheng He's treasure fleet continues its voyages across the Indian Ocean, establishing Chinese naval dominance and tributary relationships. By 1442, the fleet has made multiple voyages to East Africa, the Arabian Peninsula, and the Indian Ocean rim. These expeditions are sponsored by the Ming Dynasty and represent the height of Chinese maritime power before the shift toward isolationism in the mid-15th century.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)
Ma Huan, Yingya Shenglan (Overall Survey of the Ocean's Shores)

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's voyages are well-documented in Ming records, but the specific activities of 1442 are less clearly attested than the earlier voyages. The fleet was likely still active in this year, but the exact nature of its activities is uncertain.
cross_references: Part of broader Zheng He's seven voyages (1405–1433)

events.title: Gutenberg's early printing experiments
events.description: Johannes Gutenberg is conducting early experiments with movable-type printing in Mainz. By 1442, he is likely developing the key innovations that will lead to the printing press, including the use of metal type, oil-based ink, and the printing mechanism. The exact timeline of his innovations remains debated, but by the mid-1440s, he had likely perfected the technology.

sources.name: Gutenberg's legal documents and contracts (Mainz archives)
Later accounts by Johann Trithemius and others

events.certainty_note: The exact date of Gutenberg's innovations is uncertain. Documents place him in Mainz by 1444, but the development of the press likely occurred over several years (1440–1455). The 1442 date is speculative; the technology was probably not fully mature until the early 1450s.
cross_references: Led to 1455-001 (Printing of the Gutenberg Bible)

events.title: Portuguese exploration of the African coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, sponsored by Prince Henry the Navigator, continue systematic exploration of the West African coast. By 1442, Portuguese caravels have established trading posts and forts along the coast, including the fortress of Arguin. These expeditions are motivated by the search for gold, spices, and a sea route to Asia, as well as the conversion of African peoples to Christianity. The voyages mark the beginning of European colonial expansion in Africa and the Portuguese slave trade.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Portuguese royal patron)

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery of Guinea

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration is well-documented in royal records and contemporary chronicles. The establishment of the fortress of Arguin is dated to 1442 in some sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Age of Exploration (1415 onwards)

events.title: Council of Constance reforms implementation
events.description: The reforms of the Council of Constance (1414–1418) continue to be implemented unevenly across Christendom. The council had attempted to reform the Church and resolve the Western Schism, but its decrees are being resisted by various ecclesiastical authorities. By 1442, the implementation of these reforms is incomplete, and tensions between papal authority and conciliar authority remain unresolved.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus from Church history scholarship

events.certainty_note: The implementation of Council of Constance reforms is well-documented in ecclesiastical records, but the specific state of implementation in 1442 is less clearly attested. The general trend of uneven implementation is well-established.
cross_references: Part of broader Church reform movement (1414–1517)

disconfirming_evidence: Alfonso V's conquest of Naples is sometimes portrayed as a triumph of Renaissance culture, but it was achieved through military force and the displacement of the Angevin dynasty. The conquest did not immediately bring peace to Naples; internal conflicts and resistance to Aragonese rule continued. Zheng He's voyages are sometimes presented as evidence of Chinese maritime superiority, but the Ming Dynasty's subsequent shift toward isolationism suggests that the voyages were not seen as economically or strategically beneficial by later rulers. The Council of Constance reforms are often presented as a success, but their uneven implementation and the continued tensions between papal and conciliar authority suggest that the council's goals were not fully achieved.
historiographic_note: The year 1442 is moderately well-documented in European sources (Aragonese, Neapolitan, Portuguese, papal records) but less thoroughly in Asian sources available to Western scholarship. The conquest of Naples is extensively recorded in Aragonese and Neapolitan archives. Chinese records are rich but often filtered through later compilations. African perspectives on Portuguese exploration are almost entirely absent from contemporary written sources; the African side of these encounters is reconstructed from later oral traditions and archaeological evidence. The historiography of this period is heavily shaped by European state records; the experiences of common people, women, and non-elite groups are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Alfonso V's conquest of Naples established Aragonese power in the Mediterranean, which was consolidated through the Union of Aragon and Castile in 1479.
Zheng He's continued voyages in 1442 led to the final voyage's return in 1444.
Gutenberg's early printing experiments in 1442 led to the printing of the Gutenberg Bible circa 1455.
Portuguese exploration of the African coast in 1442 continued and accelerated in subsequent years.
The uneven implementation of Council of Constance reforms in 1442 contributed to the tensions that led to the Protestant Reformation in 1517.
